Why TUIs are back

Terminal user interfaces have been around for decades, but they're seeing a renaissance in modern developer tooling. Tools like lazygit, k9s, and helix have shown that a well-designed TUI can rival graphical applications for productivity, especially for tasks that involve text editing, file navigation, or rapid iteration.

The shift toward TUIs reflects a broader trend in the developer community: tools that respect the terminal as a first-class environment, rather than treating it as a fallback. This is a return to roots — early Unix had thoughtful TUIs like vi and emacs — but with modern affordances like mouse support, true-color rendering, and asynchronous IO that lets the interface stay responsive while background work runs.

What makes a TUI work in 2026? It needs to be discoverable (keybindings shown in a status line), composable (works alongside other shell tools), and fast (sub-frame response to keystrokes). The new generation hits those marks.
